Appearance
Text
This page always uses small font size
Width
The content is as wide as possible for your browser window.
Color (beta)
This page is always in light mode.
Shortcuts: WD:PC, WD:CHAT, WD:?
From Wikidata
Latest comment: 2 hours ago by Bovlb in topic Non-responsive user adding incorrect info - quickstatements
Wikidata project chat
A place to discuss any and all aspects of Wikidata: the project itself, policy and proposals, individual data items, technical issues, etc.

Please use {{Q}} or {{P}} the first time you mention an item or property, respectively.
Other places to find help

  • Frequently asked questions
  • Requests for deletions
  • Merging instructions
  • Report a technical problem
  • Requests for protections, undeletions and other matters requiring administrator attention. To report vandalism, click here.
For realtime chat rooms about Wikidata, see Wikidata:IRC.
Start a new discussion

Edit
On this page, old discussions are archived after 7 days. An overview of all archives can be found at this page's archive index. The current archive is located at 2026/05.
SpBot archives all sections tagged with {{Section resolved|1=~~~~}} after 1 day and sections whose oldest comment is older than 7 days.

Mix'n'Match conundrum

[edit]
Latest comment: 5 days ago2 comments1 person in discussion

I created https://mix-n-match.toolforge.org/#/catalog/7918 Mix'n'Match catalogue 7918] a few days ago. After several hours, the 'auxiliary data matcher' automatically matched ~33% of the 9K entries, but since then nothing. There is not a single "preliminarily match", even though I have found dozens of matches with a single search result on Wikidata.

Why are there no preliminarily matches? Andy Mabbett (Pigsonthewing); Talk to Andy; Andy's edits 10:56, 21 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

A couple of days ago, about 3K preliminary matches appeared, and I and others have been working through them. Overnight (UK time), the figure has gone back to zero, with no new matches confirmed. Andy Mabbett (Pigsonthewing); Talk to Andy; Andy's edits 10:31, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Is VIAF stable?

[edit]
Latest comment: 6 days ago4 comments3 people in discussion

I've been using VIAF for months for mapping between identifiers of people. However, yesterday I discovered https://viaf.org/en/viaf/43566813 and https://web.archive.org/web/20250826124853/https://viaf.org/en/viaf/43566813 and am now questioning everything I though I knew about VIAF. I just can't see how this is a stable identifier. Stuartyeates (talk) 19:53, 22 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

There seems to be something wrong with the archive links - it has 43566813 in the URL, but the page contains "VIAF ID: 232259786" and a permalink for that ID. Clicking on the permalink took me to https://web.archive.org/web/20250829042608/https://viaf.org/en/viaf/232259786, a page that shows the information for VIAF ID 24868758; the permalink there took me to VIAF ID 97105654. Peter James (talk) 20:18, 22 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
I am not sure how widespread that is but there are some examples of VIAF clusters getting repurposed, like https://viaf.org/en/viaf/59183378 started for Drew Fudenberg (Q1258707): American economist and is now for the First National Bank of Lynn. --Thomas Kerboul (BGE) (talk) 08:19, 23 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
I've just been and viewed a dozen VIAF pages on archive.org, all of them seem be for the wrong number. I'm guessing that there's javascript involved and it's breaking things. Stuartyeates (talk) 09:23, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Latest comment: 4 days ago2 comments2 people in discussion

Is Q138709837 the same person as John-Theophanes Papademetriou (Q138709849) (in which case we should merge them), or a different person whose Greek name is coincidentally the same (in which case we should probably delete Q138709837, because it just has a Greek label and no indication of significance)? TTWIDEE (talk) 19:54, 22 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

The first one is just empty - it has a Greek label and human. Nothing links to it and no derived statements. We could merge or delete. Secretlondon (talk) 12:36, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

How do I move a value to the first position?

[edit]
Latest comment: 5 days ago10 comments5 people in discussion

Hello, I’m new to Wikidata. I’m trying to move the new logo on the Google Forms page to the first position, but I can’t do it. I tried a bit but it didn’t work. Could someone help me? EDCrasher (talk) 14:36, 23 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Hi there. Wikidata doesn't sort claims in the user interface. If the order is significant, it is specified with "start time" and "end time" as you did on Google Forms (Q47014869). Alternatively "point in time" or "series ordinal" for things that are sorted on something other than a point in time. When querying for this data, it can be requested in sorted order. Infrastruktur (talk) 15:46, 23 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Hello, thank you for your response. Could you please correct this for me? I made 5 changes on the same page, so I’m worried it might be misunderstood.
First row (top value): Google Forms icon (2026)
Second row: Google Forms 2020 logo
Third row: Google Forms logo (2014–2020) EDCrasher (talk) 15:55, 23 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
EDCrasher, you may be looking for Rearrange Values, a gadget which adds a button for reordering the values of a property, if the property has more than one value. You can select it in the gadgets section of your preferences at Special:Preferences. TSventon (talk) 16:09, 23 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
No, that’s not what I’m looking for. I want to change the order of the logos. EDCrasher (talk) 16:14, 23 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
EDCrasher, try it. Select Rearrange Values and save, open the item and you should see a blue button underneath "logo image". Click the button and you should see up and down arrows to the right of each logo and a save button to the left. Move the logos to your preferred order and click save. TSventon (talk) 16:47, 23 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Unbelievable, this worked. Thank you so much. I made a total of 6 changes on the page, I hope it wasn’t misunderstood :( EDCrasher (talk) 16:51, 23 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Why do you want to move the logo? If you're trying to represent is as the most current version, you might want to look at ranking. Bovlb (talk) 15:16, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Yes, you are right I could have used the ranks. However, on the Wikidata pages for Google Slides and Google Sheets, the most up to date logos are displayed at the top. I also wanted to arrange the logos on the Google Forms page in the same way as on the Slides and Sheets pages. EDCrasher (talk) 15:22, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
E.g. some scrtipts takes first good value and uses it. SO is better to have it preferred or first. JAn Dudík (talk) 09:01, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Talk:Q7796176 ~2026-31110-55 (talk) 21:11, 23 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

I'd wait until en:WP decide what to do. If there is an entry there we need to have one here. Vicarage (talk) 10:59, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Request to replace Asturian term "*estauxunidense" with "d'Estaos Xuníos"

[edit]
Latest comment: 3 hours ago4 comments2 people in discussion

Hello! I request that the term "estauxunidense" be changed in the description field for the Asturian language to "d'Estaos Xuníos". This term does not exist in Asturian language (it's a Wikipedia creation; not registered in "DALLA" or "DGLA", the two principal dictionaries for Asturian language - the former is the standard). The correct and valid demonym for the United States is either "americanu/americana" or "norteamericanu/norteamericana" ([1], and in Diccionariu de consultes llingüístiques del asturianu, Ramón d'Andrés, Ediciones Trabe, 2021, ISBN 978-84-18286-55-1). Since there are over 300,000 records and Asturian language distinguishes between masculine and feminine, and the words "americanu" and "norteamericanu" can be ambiguous (is it for the country or for the continent?), the most accurate option and valid form is "d'Estaos Xuníos", proposed term in Nomes de los países del mundu y de les sos capitales y xentilicios, Academia de la Llingua Asturiana, 2010, ISBN 978-84-8168-500-8. Thank you. Limotecariu (talk) 07:36, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

I can do this task, but I will submit a bot request for it, as it seems to involve changing a large number of items, and I can tidy up labels at the same time. William Avery (talk) 12:02, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Ok! Thank you! Limotecariu (talk) 19:58, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
I have filed Wikidata:Requests for permissions/Bot/William Avery Bot 13 at WD:BRFA. William Avery (talk) 13:48, 30 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Latest comment: 6 days ago1 comment1 person in discussion

The iNaturalist (Q16958215) website seems to have been updated with new entries. At some point the property iNaturalist taxon ID (P3151) was populated from their website's API(?) data. Could another run be made to get the new plant IDs onto Wikidata? Abductive (talk) 09:55, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Merge or distinguish: Q177719 (medical diagnosis) vs Q9206884 (medical diagnostics)

[edit]
Latest comment: 6 days ago3 comments3 people in discussion

Looking for community input on whether Q177719 medical diagnosis (79 sitelinks, enwiki Medical diagnosis) and Q9206884 medical diagnostics (36 sitelinks, fawiki تشخیص پزشکی, frwiki Diagnostic (médecine)) should be merged.

Both items carry identical labels in several languages (e.g. Persian «تشخیص پزشکی»), and the English Wikipedia article treats diagnosis-the-result and diagnostics-the-practice as a single topic.

However, 19 wikis maintain separate articles for the two — ar, ban, bs, ca, commons, es, et, eu, hr, hy, kk, lt, lv, pl, ru, sh, sr, uk, uz — so Special:MergeItems refuses the operation, and the split may actually reflect a real ontological distinction (result vs. practice).

Two questions:

  1. Are these genuinely duplicates per Wikidata convention, or is the diagnosis (result) / diagnostics (practice) distinction worth preserving?
  2. If duplicates, which is the correct merge target? Q177719 is older and richer; Q9206884 holds fawiki/frwiki/itwiki/ptwiki/ruwiki.

Full background on the item talk pages: Talk:Q177719#Possible merge with the parallel diagnosis/diagnostics item and Talk:Q9206884#Possible merge with the parallel diagnosis/diagnostics item. Pinging Wikidata:WikiProject Medicine. Mamouri (talk) 11:16, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

19 wikis maintain separate articles for the two is probably a solid justification for the diagnosis (result) / diagnostics (practice) distinction worth preserving. Identical labels can be corrected. --Matěj Suchánek (talk) 11:41, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
They are separate concepts. Secretlondon (talk) 11:43, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

I was previously told here it's okay to use the Project chat for merge requests. --Like the windows (talk) 22:22, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
That's fine. I favour "teach someone to fish" over "give someone a fish" whenever possible. :) Bovlb (talk) 22:48, 24 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
No problem. So... can you merge them then? --Like the windows (talk) 06:29, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

[edit]
Latest comment: 5 days ago2 comments2 people in discussion

I was exploring tourism related entries from Pakistan recently and noticed that some hospitality businesses still have very limited structured information available. While checking a few boutique hotels in Islamabad, I saw that details like coordinates, official websites, amenities, and historical property data are often incomplete or inconsistent across entries. One example I came across was Grace Hospitality, which made me wonder how contributors here usually verify and organize hotel related data for local tourism coverage. Is there a preferred property structure or formatting approach for boutique hotels in Islamabad and similar regional hospitality listings on Wikidata? Mohsin879 (talk) 09:51, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

It's very unlikely that these hotels meet our notability criteria. Secretlondon (talk) 11:36, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Q599151 Official = Q108290289 senior government official, or = something else ?

[edit]
Latest comment: 2 days ago8 comments3 people in discussion

I could use some help on official (Q599151) which appears to be conflating different meanings drawn from different languages. (Or is it?)

Is it a synonym for senior government officials (Q108290289) (item for ISCO-08 occupation class (P8283) = 1112 ("senior government official") From the item descriptions in English, French, and German it would seem so.

But somebody's actually given it ISCO-08 occupation class (P8283) = 4 ("clerical support worker"). ((in error? or motivated by one of the articles or translations? I've deprecated it for the time being))

Some of the linked articles do seem significantly wider -- eg en-wiki starts "An official is someone who holds an office ([meaning position]) in an organization or government and participates in the exercise of authority" -- seemingly including 'petty officials', and non-government roles.

The English description was maybe closer to this until February, when User:GrandEscogriffe changed it (diff). To be fair though the old version wasn't very clear.

What's to be done here? Is senior government officials (Q108290289) properly a subclass of official (Q599151) (since the latter are perhaps not necessarily "senior") ? Or are they merge candidates? Or should an item created to match an ISCO class be left un-merged, to make sure it remains a SKOS exact match (Q39893449) for the ISCO class, rather than a perhaps slightly looser wiki concept ? Or should such items be merged wherever possible to try to keep our subclass of (P279) tree as tight as possible?

Is the scope note from AAT Q599151#P1014 compatible with labels of "Altos funcionarios" / "Hauts fonctionnaires" / "hoher Beamter, Oberbeamter, Spitzenbeamter" from es, fr, de linked vocabularies ?

Is the topic's main category (P910) match to Category:Officials (Q7718825) correct -- the wikilinks all seem to go to things like fr:Catégorie:Haut fonctionnaire.

I would welcome any thoughts. -- Jheald (talk) 11:34, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Teolemon
Netoholic
econterms
Jneubert
Moebeus
Albertvillanovadelmoral
Epìdosis

Notified participants of WikiProject Occupations and professions -- Jheald (talk) 11:39, 25 May 2026 (UTC) Reply

Hello Jheald. Thank you for tacking this. I think you are right that the item currently conflates meanings from different languages. Currently, the labels in French, German, Spanish, and Catalan (and maybe other languages) have an adjective explicitly stating that the item is about "high" offices. That is why I edited the English description this way. I am open to settling on a broader definition of the item, and editing the labels and descriptions accordingly. But we should not revert to the previous English description, which was really incomprehensible to me.
In any case, it seems that much work has to be done, including reordering the interwikis. For example en:Official and fr:Haute fonction publique probably should not be interwikis of each other, and the latter (and maybe other interwikis) should be moved to high civil servant (Q105434600). GrandEscogriffe (talk) 15:43, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Thanks GrandEscogriffe. high civil servant (Q105434600) is a good find. What do you think about high civil servant (Q105434600) and senior government officials (Q108290289) ? Jheald (talk) 12:08, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
They seem to refer to the same real-world occupations. The latter item is specifically tied to an ISCO-08 category, and therefore its labels are written in bureaucratese. I favor merging. Items that exist only in relation to one particular external database are bad modelling in my opinion, they should be as connected to the broader Wikidata environment as possible. GrandEscogriffe (talk) 16:42, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Do we have a WikiProject for thesauruses (in the sense of thesaurus (Q17152639) ? I was looking for a project to project-ping to get thoughts on the question of thesaurus group-entries like senior government officials (Q108290289), but couldn't identify one. Jheald (talk) 12:18, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Given the high number of uses of the item, it is necessary to proceed cautiously, so it is not appropriate to change its meaning. I think the original meaning really was ordinary official (clerk) rather than senior official, and that is how it should remain. The descriptions in many languages (and use of item) also still seem to correspond to this. Jklamo (talk) 15:43, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
@Jklamo: So would you think the French-language sitelinks should be moved (and a handful of other languages) as per GrandEscogriffe above, but keep the item (and what links to it) otherwise pretty much the same ?
Note - because of other commitments, I'm not going to be able to give this any more time for the next week or so. But anybody feel free to go ahead and do what you think should be done, if we can find agreement here. Jheald (talk) 23:21, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Wikidata weekly summary #733

[edit]
Latest comment: 3 days ago2 comments2 people in discussion
Here's your quick overview of what has been happening around Wikidata in the
week leading up to 2026-05-25. Missed the previous one? See issue #732.

Discussions

  • New requests for permissions/Bot: Skolkoll-bot 1 - Task(s): Adding Skolverket identifiers and source URLs to Swedish school items

Events

  • Upcoming events: Wikicafé May Edition: De Wikidata à Wikipédia abstraite : promesses et illusions du savoir structuré (From Wikidata to Abstract Wikipedia: Promises and Illusions of Structured Knowledge), Presentation by Jsamwrites, May 26, 2026 at 13:00 video link, organized by DMontagne en résidence.

Press, articles, blog posts, videos

  • Papers: Anna Clara Maniero Azzolini, Pietro Ortimini, and Camillo Carlo Pellizzari di San Girolamo, Wikibase and Wikidata for Greek and Latin Epigraphy: Modelling, Analysing, and Making Reusable Linked Open Data
  • Videos: Monica Berti and Camillo Carlo Pellizzari di San Girolamo, Wikidata Queries for Greek and Latin Philology (SunoikisisDC, Summer 2026); slides on Commons + video on Youtube; for more information, cf. the WikiProjects Antiquity/Pauly-Wissowa and LAGL

Tool of the week

  • LexeMap - is a lexeme coverage mapper that audits Wikidata lexeme data across languages. It runs live SPARQL queries against Wikidata and cross-references against Wiktionary to identify gaps in lexicographical data.

Other Noteworthy Stuff

  • Starting May 28, 2026, the Special:EntityData page will no longer support the PHP‑serialized format (e.g .php URLs). Affected requests will return HTTP errors. Please switch to a stable format like JSON (example: Q42.json). The change is already active on test and beta wikis. (full announcement)

Newest properties and property proposals to review

  • Newest External identifiers: ‎SIK-ISEA document ID, identifiant d'un fonds au catalogue collectif de France, ‎StaphBase ID
  • New General datatypes property proposals to review:
    • PCBS Locality code (Locality Code of the Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics)
    • nameplate image (image of a name-plate or similar identifying plate of the subject)
    • photographer (photographer)
  • New External identifier property proposals to review: Identifiant Bibliothèques patrimoniales de Paris, CUEAnexo, Enciclopedia Católica Wikitólica ID, Adult Film Database film series ID, Adult Film Database studio ID, Satker ID, Inaproc Instance ID, British Libray catalogue ID, Swedish-Danish Dictionary ID, Roller-results.com skater ID

You can comment on all open property proposals!

Did you know?

  • Query examples: People born at the sea (source)
  • Newest database reports: List of items with links to Wiktionary main space.
  • Showcase Items: Boeing(Q66)

Development

  • Mobile statement editing:
    • We continued working on reducing the page size to make it easier on mobile data plans
    • We are improving how error messages are shown
  • Making WikiProjects more visible: We are continuing the work on getting it ready for a first rollout.

You can see all open tickets related to Wikidata here. If you want to help, you can also have a look at the tasks needing a volunteer.

Weekly Tasks

  • Add labels, in your own language(s), for the new properties listed above.
  • Contribute to the showcase Item and Lexeme above.
  • Govdirectory weekly focus country:
  • Summarize your WikiProject's ongoing activities in one or two sentences.
  • Help translate or proofread the interface and documentation pages, in your own language!
  • Help merge identical items across Wikimedia projects.
  • Help write the next summary!
· Previous issue · Full report · Unsubscribe . JosefAnthony talk ·MediaWiki message delivery (talk) 15:29, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
LexeMap, or WikiLexAudit 4.0 as the title bar says, seems to be missing some languages that have lexicographic data (example: ldn) Arlo Barnes (talk) 19:28, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Consensus for Kurdish (ckb) descriptions of provinces in the Kurdistan region

[edit]
Latest comment: 18 hours ago46 comments18 people in discussion

Following the advice of User:Bovlb, I am starting this discussion to establish a formal community consensus regarding the descriptions in the Kurdish (ckb) language field for provinces and cities in the Kurdistan region.

The Proposal

[edit]

As the Central Kurdish (ckb) community, including its administrators and active contributors, we propose that the descriptions for these geographic entities in our language field should strictly reflect our linguistic and geographical terminology. The established consensus among our native speakers is to use the following regional descriptions:

  • For provinces in the north: "Province in Northern Kurdistan" (پارێزگایەک لە باکووری کوردستان).
  • For provinces in the east: "Province in East Kurdistan" (پارێزگایەک لە ڕۆژهەڵاتی کوردستان).
  • For provinces in the west: "Province in West Kurdistan" (پارێزگایەک لە ڕۆژاوای کوردستان).
  • For provinces in the south: "Province in South Kurdistan" (پارێزگایەک لە باشووری کوردستان).

Rationale

[edit]

1. Linguistic and Geographic Identity: In the Kurdish language (ckb), these areas are historically and geographically identified solely by these regional names. Our community has the absolute right to use its own native terminology in its specific language field to reflect its identity.

2. Project Consistency: This terminology is the standard used across the Central Kurdish Wikipedia (ckbwiki) and all Kurdish academic and media sources. We aim to maintain consistency across all Wikimedia projects in our language.

We request that the Wikidata community respect the linguistic autonomy of the ckb field and the consensus of its native speakers and administrators. We also request external users to refrain from interfering with these fields, as they represent the consensus of the local community.

I am notifying BSRF of this discussion. Tessla (talk) 16:14, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

 Strong support: This terminology is the established community consensus in the Kurdish language (ckb). ‏‎ʜᴀʀʏᴀᴅ ᴛᴀʟᴋ ‏‎ 19:53, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
 Strong support:I support the Kurdish Wikipedia community, and this is how we, as Kurds, read and interpret it.1silent scientist (talk) 20:28, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
 Strong support: This terminology is the established community consensus in the Kurdish language (ckb).~2026-31469-65 (talk) 20:37, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
  •  Strong support: This is the standard terminology across Kurdish dialects. I don't see how this is an issue.--Épine (talk) 22:37, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
 Strong support: This terminology is the established community consensus in the Kurdish language (ckb). – The preceding unsigned comment was added by Saad Goran (talk • contribs) at 22:59, 25 May 2026 (UTC).Reply
 SupportThis doesn't need voting or consensus. This is the wording used in the CKB community, which is backed up by numerous reliable sources. For the reasons mentioned above this wording is very much correct and in line with what is used inside the ckb Wiki. As an administrator in the CKB community I can testify to what is shared by user Tessla. Pirehelokan (talk) 04:37, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
 Strong support The descriptions used above are geographical descriptions. We, as the Wikipedia Community that speaks and writes Northern Kurdish, also use them to designate regions. I want to state that censoring these descriptions is absolutely not a good-faith action. ~2026-30231-25 (talk) 05:38, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
 Strong support This terminology is the established community consensus in the Kurdish language (ckb). Ari Qasm (talk) 07:24, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
  • First of all, Wikimedia projects are not democracies and polling is not a substitute for discussion. It's funny how these people (especially unregistered users with no prior contribution) cast a vote without a proper argument. Statements like "we are Kurds, this is how we do" are not credible. I know why Kurdish users swarm this discussion. User:Tessla, the one who started this discussion, sent a message on ckbwiki and called for users to vote: "As you know, there has been a problem in Wikidata in recent days about the description of the Kurdish language (ckb) for the provinces of North Kurdistan. A Turkish user constantly reverts our edits and is disrespectful to our project managers. After the official complaint, with the guidance of the Wikidata administrators, I have started an official discussion in Project Chat to show our consensus as a Kurdish Wikipedia community and prevent these external interferences. Please all go to this link and vote below:' How do I vote?' Just put the Support template at the bottom of the message and then write next to it: This terminology is the established community consensus in the Kurdish language (ckb). Please see this as a national and cultural duty to protect the sovereignty of our language in global projects." Such a pathetic move! This constitutes canvassing. Bovlb, is this process legitimate? Content disputes should be resolved through discussion, consensus-building, and input from uninvolved third parties.
Tessla says that they followed the advice of User:Bovlb. Bovlb pointed out that "it is common practice on Wikidata for descriptions of geographic entities to include the name of the country". Description sections of items must be written from a neutral point of view (NPOV). NPOV is a fundamental principle of all Wikimedia projects. Central Kurdish Wikipedia (ckbwiki) does not have the right to be exempt of this non-negotiable policy.
Ckbwiki has a broader neutrality problem. They list some provinces of Turkey, Iraq, Iran and Syria under the flag of Iraqi Kurdistan. This is a case of irredentism. They call 2024 Turkish local elections as "Regional Elections in North Kurdistan and Turkey (2024). They use the term "North Kurdistan" like it's a different political entity. That's why its admins and users cannot comprehend neutral point of view.
A similar situation was present on Azerbaijani Wikipedia a few years ago. They used to call Armenian Genocide as "Qondarma Erməni soyqırımı" (Fake Armenian Genocide). In 2019 stewards intervened following a "RFC discussion on Meta-Wiki". If ckbwiki does not comply with NPOV, another intervention might occur.--BSRF 💬 08:06, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
I will address the points raised regarding policy and conduct:
  1. Notification vs. Canvassing: The message on ckbwiki was a neutral notification to the Kurdish community about a discussion that directly affects their language field (ckb). Informing a linguistic community that their expertise is needed is standard practice for transparency. The users participating here are long-time, trusted contributors and administrators who are expressing a genuine linguistic consensus, not a coordinated "meatpuppets" campaign.
  2. Linguistic Autonomy: The Kurdish language field (ckb) must reflect how these regions are identified by its native speakers. Our community consensus is that the geographic identity as "Northern/Eastern/etc. Kurdistan" is the primary and most accurate way to describe these entities in our language. Wikidata should respect the autonomy of native communities to define their own descriptions.
  3. Addressing False Analogies: Comparing this linguistic discussion to historical negationism or past cross-wiki conflicts is an inflammatory and irrelevant generalization. We are discussing the standard terminology of a living language as used in our project (ckbwiki) and academic sources.
  4. Respecting Local Consensus: Wikidata policy values the input of native speakers and local project administrators. Dismissing the voices of multiple established administrators and contributors as "nationalistic" simply because they agree on their own language terminology is not a constructive way to build consensus.

The strength of this proposal lies in the unanimous consensus of the ckb community, which should be the primary consideration for this language field. Tessla (talk) 10:22, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

"Please see this as a national and cultural duty to protect the sovereignty of our language in global projects."? Secretlondon (talk) 12:33, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
BSRF brings up valid points. Consensus is not a simple majority, agreed. And a consensus on kurdish wikipedia does not translate beyond kurdish wikipedia either. It also would seem that Wikidata's poor approach to policies also means there is no safeguards against "outsiders" collaborating to force a consensus based on people who may not be active on Wikidata. This has happened before, but this is the only time it really have been questioned AFAIK, kind of sad actually. Principles from english Wikipedia is a useful guide for sure, but technically only global policies and Wikidata policies are valid.
They forget that consensus is what makes our policies valid in the first place, so it could potentially override most policies we have. There is no minimum requirement to vote on things other than high-level RFPs so there's little stopping kurdish wikipedia from pushing their will. Except maybe canvassing? This is where it gets dicey. Not formally a Wikidata rule AFAIK but still understood to be a guiding principle.
After looking at the guidance in Help:Descriptions at first I was skeptical whether there was a real controversy regarding the control over the territories in question, but after reading more about it personally I have changed my mind. Why? Iraqi kurds voted 98.9% for independence. There have also been willingness to use force to assert control - so it seems to me that the kurds claims to these areas are legitimately contested and therefore "controversial". Thus our rule against controversial descriptions would apply. Infrastruktur (talk) 12:47, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
If you came here because someone asked you to, or you read a message on another website, please note that this is not a majority vote, but instead a discussion among Wikidata contributors. Agreement is gauged based on the merits of the arguments, not by counting votes.
However, you are invited to participate and your opinion is welcome. Remember to assume good faith on the part of others and to sign your posts on this page by adding ~~~~ at the end.

Bovlb (talk) 14:27, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

@Tessla : It is good to publicize discussions for the benefit of interested parties, but it is important that such notices be neutral, or it risks being seen as canvassing. I do not think it is reasonable to describe this notice as neutral. Also, if I understand you correctly, there was a prior discussion at CKB Wikipedia that established a local consensus on this issue; it would be helpful to include a link.
@BSRF: Please tone down your rhetoric and focus on the content issue, rather than making comments about other editors. Your comparisons are not helpful in resolving this issue.
Turning to the content issue: I am not a ckb speaker, nor am I an expert on this region, but it seems to me that both the long-standing Kurdish cultural identity and the internationally-recognized location within Turkey are important to understanding the context of these provinces. Why are we not considering a compromise solution like "province in Northern Kurdistan, Turkey"? Bovlb (talk) 14:48, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
There is one point I made in the AN thread that may be worth stating explicitly here to avoid confusion: Language-specific Wikimedia projects are generally given substantial deference regarding language usage and local conventions, but they do not formally control Wikidata descriptions in that language.
For example, English Wikipedia is a large project with many documented conventions, but it does not determine the conventions for English descriptions on Wikidata, particularly given that multiple client projects use English. The same principle applies here, even though CKB Wikipedia is currently the only client project for that language. The views of CKB Wikipedia contributors are highly relevant and deserve serious consideration, but consensus on Wikidata is ultimately determined here through discussion here. Bovlb (talk) 17:18, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
The contexts are Turkey, Iran, Syria and Iraq. It is possible that different compromises will be needed for different regions. TSventon (talk) 15:37, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Thank you, Bovlb, for your mediation and for seeking a solution. I would like to address the current state of the discussion:
  1. Regarding the compromise (Northern Kurdistan, Turkey): I have consulted with the Kurdish community and administrators. The consensus remains very firm: in the Kurdish language (ckb), adding the state name to the regional description is seen as alien to our linguistic identity. In our textbooks, media, and daily discourse, these provinces are identified solely as "Northern Kurdistan" (and respectively for other parts). We believe the Kurdish language field should reflect how native speakers naturally identify these regions.
  2. Linguistic Autonomy: Our position is that the description field in a specific language should be reserved for the terminology of that language community. The political status of these regions is already acknowledged in other data fields of the item, so there is no need to repeat it in the ckb description in a way that contradicts our local linguistic standards.
  3. Community Participation: The Kurdish users and administrators participating here are established contributors who are concerned about their language. Their presence is a legitimate expression of community consensus on a matter that directly affects the ckb field, rather than "canvassing."
  4. Conclusion: We ask for respect for the autonomy of the ckb field. We are not here to push a political agenda, but to ensure that our language is represented accurately according to our own established standards and consensus. Tessla (talk) 16:52, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Provinces are first-level administrative divisions of Turkey. They can only be created when a law is passed in the Turkish parliament. And yet you claim that "adding the state name to the regional description is seen as alien". Cultural regions are not divided into administrative units. Not mentioning the name of Turkey in the description section of an item about a province of Turkey is against NPOV and is not acceptable. Your rejection of Bovlb's proposal shows that you don't want to find a common ground.--BSRF 💬 10:21, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Normally descriptions for cities include the state that asserts control. Which state does the citizens pay their taxes to? But if a territory is legitimately contested such descriptions are considered controversial according to Help:Descriptions. The policy says to avoid such descriptions. A new consensus here could change how kurdish descriptions are treated though. Infrastruktur (talk) 13:38, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
One question I'm still struggling with here is: Why this is being framed purely as a linguistic issue? If the concern is that Northern Kurdistan is disputed territory, and it is non-neutral or offensive to associate it with Turkey, then why is that specific to ckb descriptions? Why isn't that true across all languages? How do we handle this for other disputed territory like Palestine, Crimea, Northern Ireland, and the Falkland Islands? Bovlb (talk) 15:05, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
It is being framed as a linguistic issue because admitting it's actually a political issue is against NPOV and other Wikimedia policies. As I've pointed out above, ckbwiki lists some provinces of Turkey, Iraq, Iran and Syria under the flag of Iraqi Kurdistan. Iraq is a federal state, Turkey and others are unitary states. Those provinces have different legal systems. Flag of Iraqi Kurdistan does not represent provinces in Turkey, Iran and Syria. This is a case of irredentism.--BSRF 💬 11:08, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
  •  Comment I see a lot of merit in self-determination of label conventions by the members of a language community; however it would be good to have that documented somewhere here in Wikidata, not relying on external wikis or sources. WikiProject Kurdistan (Q17496852) exists in other wikis, can we have a home for the project here Wikidata that provides a place for Wikidata editors with an interest in the region to come to agreement on conventions like this? ArthurPSmith (talk) 19:22, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
    Thank you, ArthurPSmith, for your very constructive and insightful suggestion. I completely agree that establishing a WikiProject Kurdistan on Wikidata is an excellent way to document our linguistic conventions and local consensus. This will provide a dedicated space to resolve such terminology matters professionally and ensure consistency across our projects. I will discuss this with my fellow Kurdish administrators and contributors immediately, and we will initiate the process of creating this project here on Wikidata. We truly appreciate your support for linguistic self-determination. Tessla (talk) 09:34, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
    @Bovlb @Bovlb I suggest we start separate discussions on unrelated topics. However, removing the standard writing conventions used by the Kurdish population to refer to these disputed territories is akin to an Israeli editor changing the name “Palestine” to “Israel” on Wikidata simply because the Israeli government doesn’t recognize Palestine’s territorial integrity. Regardless of how this RFC was submitted by the user, it’s highly offensive for decisions about our language to be made by an editor with no understanding of Kurdish culture or media. We reject any and all tampering made by @BSRF which effect our projects. Épine (talk) 20:14, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
    @Épine: If I understand you correctly, you seem to be confirming that this is primarily a concern about non-neutral descriptions of disputed territory, rather than purely a question of linguistic convention. If so, then my comparison with cases like Palestine or other disputed territories does seem relevant to the discussion.
    I must also ask you to confine yourself to discussing the content issue itself and avoid making comments about other editors. Bovlb (talk) 20:33, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
    @Bovlb @Bovlb, both of those points are indeed valid. If you search for “باکووری کوردستان” or “کوردستانی باکوور” (both referring to Northern Kurdistan) on Google, you’ll find approximately half a million results. This is just in central Kurdish alone. This is evident in books, news, movies, TV shows, history books, and our education system. It’s clear that the user who engages in edit warring has a clear conflict of interest in this matter. This isn’t the first time we’ve seen such actions from these “contributors,” and it won’t be the last. Épine (talk) 08:18, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
We have a fundamental principle called "neutral point of view" (NPOV). Omitting the name of Turkey from the description section of an item about a province of Turkey is against NPOV and is not acceptable.--BSRF 💬 11:17, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
  •  Oppose I don't thing labeling populated places with ethno-regions is a propiate way to label them. We can't just label "Muslim ruled cities" for example or "Jewish town". So I don't agree. Nanahuatl (talk) 21:46, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
    @Nanahuatl this is completely unrelated and what you say does not make any sense at all. Kurdistan is the name of the region, much like Afghanistan. According to you it would be wrong to say Northern Afghanistan because is signals "etho-region"? Épine (talk) 08:20, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
I would like to clarify a key point regarding the "ethno-region" argument. Kurdistan is a well-established geographical and cultural region, recognized in historical, linguistic, and geographical contexts—much like the Balkans, Scandinavia, or the Middle East. It is not a religious or purely ethnic label as suggested.

In the Kurdish language (ckb), referring to these provinces as "Northern/Eastern Kurdistan" is the most accurate and standard geographical description. As noted by Épine, this is supported by hundreds of thousands of sources in our media and education systems. We are simply asking for our language field (ckb) to reflect this established terminology, which is the consensus of our native speakers and administrators. Tessla (talk) 09:37, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Does ckbwiki describe Varna as "oblast in the Balkans" without mentioning Bulgaria or Scania as "province in Scandinavia" without mentioning Sweden? This is what is being proposed and it sounds absurd. Also it is claimed that there was a prior local consensus on ckbwiki on this issue and Bovlb asked for a link to be provided. We are still waiting for it.--BSRF 💬 11:26, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
The consensus from the community is above right in the discussion, everyone disagreed with your disruptive editing effecting our projects. It’s also evident in the undisputed and long standing names on article discussions related to Eastern/Northern/Western Kurdistan regions. There does not need to be a Wikipedia consensus for what’s already culturally established as the appropriate terms to refer to these territories across countless sources. Consensus takes place on issues which cause division in the community, which is not the case here for us. Épine (talk) 06:32, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
----
@Bovlb Hello. To be honest, we are becoming exhausted with the lack of a clear administrative resolution here. Administrators either remain silent or provide intermittent comments without a final decision. You are administrators here, and we are administrators on the Kurdish Wikipedia; we are both office-holders whose expertise and status are recognized within the Wikimedia movement. I have shown full respect to your authority and roles, but we are still waiting for a suitable answer.
Should we proceed with establishing the WikiProject Kurdistan here on Wikidata to formalize these standards? Or is the consensus of 40–50 million Kurds—who wish to describe their own provinces in their own language field according to their established linguistic policy—not being given due weight? The Kurdish community has clearly expressed its voice in the discussion above. From this point forward, we will no longer engage with User:BSRF, as it is evident he is not interested in a constructive consensus and continues to use offensive rhetoric that does not align with our professional standards. We are waiting for a formal response from you or another Wikidata administrator.
Note to Épine: Please refrain from further replies to that user. We have stated our case clearly. Let the Wikidata administrators speak, and we will only respond to them from now on. Tessla (talk) 09:39, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
You blatantly commanding around other users like Epine reeks of authoritarianism, and suggests an unhealthy culture of fear on the kurdish Wikipedia. What gives you the authority to dictate what people can say? ~2026-32110-23 (talk) 11:15, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
@BSRF first of all remain logged in when replying to users, this constitutes as sock-puppetry. Second of all, I interpret that as a friendly note and no one is afraid of anybody. Let's be clear on this. Stop disrespecting our Wikipedia, or I will escalate. Épine (talk) 11:24, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Dropping similar discussion on the term "Southern Kurdistan" for @Bovlb solidifying user consensus on similar terminology, and the discussion right above it. Épine (talk) 11:38, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Épine It is highly contradictory to accuse another editor of manipulation while simultaneously defending a clear canvas on ckbwiki as a friendly note. Calling on users to vote as a national duty is a textbook example of meat-puppetry and vote-stacking under global Wikimedia guidelines, I am sure Wikidata administrators will see this and take action about it. Instead of launching unfounded personal attacks against others, the focus should be on following the core neutrality and community policies of this project. Threats of escalation will not change the fact that global rules apply to everyone equally, and there is absolutely no policy basis for your complaints.  Strong oppose Beyoglou (talk) 13:21, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
@Beyoglou it does not matter what the user said in their RfC. What matters is that the majority of users have highlighted their opposition to ultra-nationalists meddling in our projects. And how exactly did you find this discussion yourself? Épine (talk) 14:16, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
This is a content issue. On Wikidata, content issues are resolved by the community, not by administrative fiat. Our role here as administrators is to facilitate respectful discussion and prevent disruption.
I appreciate that you are keen to see a resolution here. Currently, I do not see a clear consensus emerging. Key questions remain unanswered. Bovlb (talk) 16:39, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
@Bovlb No consensus means preserving status quo, which is what the original values were before the external meddling. Épine (talk) 17:49, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Discussion

[edit]

It is common Wikidata practice to add the state (US: country) in geographic descriptions. The main purpose of this is to provide useful context for readers who might not immediately be familiar with administrative subdivisions (cf US state).

A reason to avoid adding the state might be if the territory is disputed, and/or if the location and status of the subdivision is well-known. In this case, most people worldwide probably don't know off-hand where Northern Kurdistan is. There is strong reason to believe, however, that knowledge about the region is substantially higher among readers of Kurdish.

It might be helpful to consider how this is handled in similar cases, even if they are not exact analogues. For example:

  • West Bank (Q36678): Palestinian territory on the western bank of the Jordan River, occupied by Israel
  • Bethlehem (Q5776): city in the State of Palestine
  • Crimea (Q7835): Eastern European peninsula, in the Black Sea and Sea of Azov, disputed between Ukraine (de jure) and Russia (de facto)
  • Alushta (Q46001): city in Crimea, Ukraine
  • Northern Ireland (Q26): part of the United Kingdom situated on the island of Ireland
  • Belfast (Q10686): capital city of Northern Ireland (but in French "ville du Royaume-Uni, capitale de l'Irlande du Nord")

Bovlb (talk) 18:49, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

As seen in the examples above, even the descriptions of disputed territories/places include country names. As far as I know Turkey's sovereignty is not disputed countrywide. No other entity has territorial claims against Turkey. There is no reason to omit Turkey's name from the description sections of items about its provinces.--BSRF 💬 20:12, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
@Bovlb I appreciate your detailed analysis. However, I want to refocus the discussion on the most important stakeholder: the Kurdish reader.
Descriptions in the Kurdish (ckb) language field are primarily meant for Kurdish speakers. For a Kurd, seeing "Turkey" attached to a province like Amed in their own language field is not just alien—it is genuinely confusing. Why? Because the Kurdish community—numbering 50 to 60 million—is immersed in a cultural and linguistic environment where these regions are exclusively identified as Northern, Southern, Eastern, or Western Kurdistan.
From television, radio, and music to newspapers, books, and daily conversation, this is the terminology we use. When a Kurdish reader encounters "Turkey" in a ckb description while every other cultural touchpoint says "Northern Kurdistan," it creates a cognitive dissonance. We feel it is our duty as administrators to protect our linguistic and cultural identity from being distorted for future generations.
We live in an age of advanced technology. I invite you to ask any modern AI model or perform a data analysis on Kurdish linguistic frequency: Does the global Kurdish community refer to these provinces as "Turkey" or "Northern Kurdistan" in their native tongue? I am so confident in this reality that if an objective data analysis or AI proves that Kurds prefer the state name in their native terminology, I will step down and accept it. But the data will show that "Northern Kurdistan" is our undeniable standard. We are simply asking for Wikidata to reflect this reality for our readers. Tessla (talk) 20:34, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
You keep forgetting about Wikimedia's fundamental principle of "neutral point of view" (NPOV). Not mentioning Turkey's name is not neutral, it's biased.--BSRF 💬 21:02, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
@BSRF and you seem to keep forgetting that what you want is simply untrue and foreign to our language. Content must reflect the reality of the audience it is designed for. I understand how this might be confusing for you, but this is simply the linguistic reality. Épine (talk) 22:48, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Large number of homonyms

[edit]
Latest comment: 4 days ago2 comments2 people in discussion

when i want to select locations for c:com:sdc (depicts or any sdc in general), it's often impractical, and i have to come to wd and use the search engine here to find it and then copy paste the q number back to commons, because often i'm looking for a street or a building that has very generic names e.g. high street, main street, market square, city hall...

either the search engine (the auto suggest feature) needs to be improved, or aliases in the format of "generic name, city/town name" should be massively added.

also, the description should be more detailed than something like "building in France" because very often there are multiple things in the same country with the same name. when i type that name, every option autosuggested has the same generic description. that's useless and frustrating. "something in city/town, country" is way more useful. RoyZuo (talk) 19:14, 25 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

I try and do this with churches as people just add them as the church name (eg St Mary's) without a location, and even the diocese doesn't call them that! The generic names are often added by bot, I agree it doesn't help disambiguate items. There is also a max of 7 or so results on commons. Secretlondon (talk) 12:30, 26 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Levee

[edit]
Latest comment: 22 hours ago8 comments4 people in discussion

I am trying to understand the difference (if any) between levee (Q138456230) and levee (Q105190). - Jmabel (talk) 02:26, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

levee (Q105190) is a subclass of levee (Q138456230). natural levee (Q11268410) is also subclass of levee (Q138456230). The descriptions do not help as the superclass is described as "natural or artificial elevated ridge on a river bank" whereas the subclass is described as "ridge or wall to hold back water", which is broader. The En Wikipedia article linked to levee (Q105190) does not really help.
Levee as a subclass of levee was created by User:Daask this year. I can see that logically natural levee is a subclass of levee, but having it as a subclass of levee (Q105190) makes more sense to me. I'm not sure what levee (Q138456230) is giving us. Secretlondon (talk) 12:14, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
@Jmabel, Secretlondon: I believe I was working on the artificial natural class hierarchy, so probably one of those was supposed to be merely for artificial versions. Daask (talk) 19:07, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Also, related, the difference (if any) between perré (Q139934476) and riprap (Q3433064). - Jmabel (talk) 02:33, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

The former seems to be a Francophone term, but the Fr wikipedia article is at riprap. I don't know if a French speaker would see a perré and a riprap as different things. Secretlondon (talk) 12:18, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
@Fralambert, Vargenau: any thoughts? - Jmabel (talk) 14:25, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
I have updated the Q139934476 element with the French article. Vargenau (talk) 14:59, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Thank you. Clearly different things with different articles on fr wikipedia. Secretlondon (talk) 20:57, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Q902508 and Q3027893

[edit]
Latest comment: 3 days ago2 comments2 people in discussion

dihydrogen cation (Q902508) and dihydrogen (Q3027893) both have UFHFLCQGNIYNRP-UHFFFAOYSA-N as their InChIKey (P235) value, while dihydrogen cation (Q902508) also has ZZIJOQHRUPVPQC-UHFFFAOYSA-N as its InChIKey (P235) value. For dihydrogen cation (Q902508), the former value has "stated in (P248): SciFinder (Q3648541)" as its reference. However, given that what SciFinder (and CAS Common Chemistry) gives as the InChI of the dihydrogen cation, InChI=1S/H2/h1H, does not have a charge layer (it has neither /q nor /p), I am not sure if SciFinder gives the correct InChI and InChIKey for the dihydrogen cation. Can this situation be sorted out? Intolerable situation (talk) 09:10, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Wikidata:WikiProject Chemistry may be better people to speak to. Secretlondon (talk) 13:54, 27 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

cannot change label : Could not save due to an error. Label and description for language code te can not have the same value.

The shop is always called Vinnies, so maybe it should be Retail Shop - "Vinnies - Australian thrift shop chain operated by St Vincent de Paul Society", and the Charity Org should be "St Vincent de Paul Society National Council of Australia - commonly known as Vinnies and Operator of the Vinnies Retail Stores" that way the two are identifiable as having a relationship but won't get the automated Fix Me and also seems more logical? For Vinnies (Q120646672) perhaps instance of charity shop, as well. The has parts of the class statement doesn't work (for me). Secretlondon (talk) 07:26, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

AutosuggestSitelink: New features available

[edit]
Latest comment: 2 days ago2 comments2 people in discussion

Hello, the

  • AutosuggestSitelink-Gadget

now has some additional features:

  1. A search form has been added, so the search term can be changed by the user (e.g. if the lemma includes parenthesis)
  2. It now suggests only disambiguation items for disambiguation pages.
  3. Changes by the tool are now tagged and can be filtered using Special:RecentChanges

The gadget can be activated like in this example:

  • meta:User:M2k~dewiki/global.js

It opens a pop-up (see example screenshot), so newly created or changed articles, which are not yet connected to a wikidata object can be connected to an object.

Unconnected pages can be found at

  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:UnconnectedPages?namespace=0&limit=1500&offset=0
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Special:WithoutInterwiki&limit=1500&offset=0
  • https://wikidata-todo.toolforge.org/duplicity/#/
    • https://wikidata-todo.toolforge.org/duplicity/#/list/enwiki
    • https://wikidata-todo.toolforge.org/duplicity/#/stats/enwiki
  • https://wikidata-game.toolforge.org/distributed/#/game/82/%7B%22type%22%3A%22all%22%7D

M2k~dewiki (talk) 12:22, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Very useful! If the tool gives an error when trying to open it, please check that the localization into your interface language is up to date. I previously noticed that the tool won't open if it is only partially translated. Samoasambia ✎ 12:30, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

How to get quickstatements.

[edit]
Latest comment: 2 days ago3 comments2 people in discussion

I really want quickstatements, I only just joined but I hope to expand the knowledge of the Hausa people and other Nigerian groups. How do I ManeHausaDescrns (talk) 12:28, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Hi! For QuickStatements access you need to became an autoconfirmed user. You will become autoconfirmed automatically after you have done at least 50 edits on Wikidata and your first edit is at least 4 days old. Samoasambia ✎ 12:32, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Ok! Thanks! ManeHausaDescrns (talk) 12:33, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Help

[edit]
Latest comment: 2 days ago2 comments2 people in discussion

While there is currently a discussion about about QuickStatement, it says this code is not working:

Q340122 TAB Dtum TAB "mulembi wa ndakatulo wa ku Poland"

Q577 TAB Dtum TAB "nyengo iyo caru capasi cikuzungulira: nyengo iyo yikughanaghanirika kuti caru capasi cizingilirenge Zuŵa ndipo yikuwoneka pa malo ghakukhazikika (pafupifupi mazuŵa 365.24); Base pamanyuma pake wakasintha kuti walongosore panji kusintha makalendala ghakupambanapambana"

How is this? I tried pressing Import it doesnt let it work. Viyowoyero-vya-Malaŵi (talk) 13:24, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

It seems to work for me (with "Import V1 commands" in QuickStatements version 2). " TAB " in the instructions represents a tab character; Q340122, Dtum and "mulembi wa ndakatulo wa ku Poland" can be separated by tabs in Notepad, or in separate cells in Excel, or pipes (|) can be used instead of tabs. The description for Q577 looks like it could be too long. Peter James (talk) 16:04, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Redundant page

[edit]
Latest comment: 1 day ago4 comments4 people in discussion

A bot just created the page AdventHealth for Children (Q139961142) which is totally redundant, when we already have an older page AdventHealth for Children (Q30264600). Catfurball (talk) 15:26, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

This seems like an issue to take up with the botop in the first instance. @Mike Peel Bovlb (talk) 16:33, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
It's PiBot, and it's because the article on en: wasn't linked to the Wikidata entry. Secretlondon (talk) 17:56, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Items have been merged. — Huntster (t @ c) 18:04, 28 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

[edit]
Latest comment: 1 day ago1 comment1 person in discussion

You are invited to voice your opinion on a new community-proposed direction for the Community Wishlist. Thank you! MediaWiki message delivery (talk) 03:07, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Vector legacy 2010 skin not scrolling top bar

[edit]
Latest comment: 1 day ago1 comment1 person in discussion

Suddenly my vector legacy 2010 skin is not scrolling the top bar away when I scroll down a page, it just over-writes the other content. I believe mediawiki did a new release this week, have they broken it? Vicarage (talk) 08:22, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Description for Azerbaijan Democratic Republic

[edit]
Latest comment: 17 hours ago6 comments5 people in discussion

@Bozqurd77 and @Yousiphh need some assistance with selecting the most useful, neutral description for Azerbaijan Democratic Republic (Q206411).

Some candidates are:

  • EN.1: the first Turkic republic in the East
  • EN.2: first modern establishment of Azerbaijan as an independent state (1918–1920)
  • EN.3: former country
  • EN.4: first Azerbaijani republic
  • TR.1: 1918–1920 yılları arasında var olan devlet (MT: The state that existed between 1918 and 1920)
  • TR.2: Doğu'da ilk Türk cumhuriyeti (MT: The First Turkic Republic in the East)

Some third-party input would be extremely helpful. Bovlb (talk) 16:05, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

I think that 3 is best. Note that if you end up using 2, you should replace the hyphen with an en dash. ―Justin (koavf)❤T☮C☺M☯ 16:17, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
Typography fixed. Bovlb (talk) 16:48, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
  • Option 1 is too ambiguous. My choice would be option 2. Option 3 is also fine but it would be better as "former country in the South Caucasus".--BSRF 💬 16:59, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
I think EN.1 and TR.2, because they are the most understandable versions. Option 3 is too general. Since the name “Azerbaijan” is already in the main title, repeating it in the description (as in EN.2 or EN.4) is unnecessary and not understandable. It would be better if we also added the South Caucasus to the versions. Bozqurd77 (talk) 17:41, 29 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
EN 3 and TR 1. EN 3 could be given more specification, i.e "former country in South Caucasus (1918–1920)" attributed to general region it stood and the time it existed, both for general categorization and general understanding. For TR 1 is most neutral IMO, attributed to general label needing the least interpretation for everybody. The more nuanced contexts like identity, history, cultural realities, should be elaborated and preserved in the more spacious place designed for containing various nuanced contexts: Wikipedia. Yamato~kun (Talk • Contribs) 00:25, 30 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

Non-responsive user adding incorrect info - quickstatements

[edit]
Latest comment: 2 hours ago2 comments2 people in discussion

Hello, User:ArasbaranIran is adding nonsense, please can someone intervene (I have tried and failed); thank you Maculosae tegmine lyncis (talk) 09:37, 30 May 2026 (UTC)Reply

User seems to be responding now. They seem to be taking part in a number of Outreach events: @King ChristLike, Lucas Piantá (WMAT), Manfred Werner (WMAT), Raimund Liebert (WMAT): Bovlb (talk) 15:12, 30 May 2026 (UTC)Reply
